title: XXXIII
html:
XXXIII

вАФпїњвАФпїњThat provision should be made for continuing the race of so great, so exalted and godlike a Being as manпїњвАФI am far from denyingпїњвАФbut philosophy speaks freely of everything; and therefore I still think and do maintain it to be a pity, that it should be done by means of a passion which bends down the faculties, and turns all the wisdom, contemplations, and operations of the soul backwardsпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњa passion, my dear, continued my father, addressing himself to my mother, which couples and equals wise men with fools, and makes us come out of our caverns and hiding-places more like satyrs and four-footed beasts than men.

I know it will be said, continued my father (availing himself of the Prolepsis), that in itself, and simply takenпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњlike hunger, or thirst, or sleepпїњвАФпїњвАФвАЩtis an affair neither good or badпїњвАФor shameful or otherwise.пїњвАФпїњвАФпїњWhy then did the delicacy of Diogenes and Plato so recalcitrate against it? and wherefore, when we go about to make and plant a man, do we put out the candle? and for what reason is it, that all the parts thereofпїњвАФthe congredientsпїњвАФthe preparationsпїњвАФthe instruments, and whatever serves thereto, are so held as to be conveyed to a cleanly mind by no language, translation, or periphrasis whatever?

вАФпїњвАФпїњThe act of killing and destroying a man, continued my father, raising his voiceпїњвАФand turning to my uncle TobyпїњвАФyou see, is gloriousпїњвАФand the weapons by which we do it are honourableпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњWe march with them upon our shouldersпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњWe strut with them by our sidesпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњWe gild themпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњWe carve themпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњWe inlay themпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњWe enrich themпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњNay, if it be but a scoundrel cannon, we cast an ornament upon the breach of it.пїњвАФ

вАФпїњвАФпїњMy uncle Toby laid down his pipe to intercede for a better epithetпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњand Yorick was rising up to batter the whole hypothesis to piecesпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњ

вАФпїњвАФпїњWhen Obadiah broke into the middle of the room with a complaint, which cried out for an immediate hearing.

The case was this:

My father, whether by ancient custom of the manor, or as impropriator of the great tythes, was obliged to keep a Bull for the service of the Parish, and Obadiah had led his cow upon a pop-visit to him one day or other the preceding summerпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњI say, one day or otherпїњвАФbecause as chance would have it, it was the day on which he was married to my fatherвАЩs housemaidпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњso one was a reckoning to the other. Therefore when ObadiahвАЩs wife was brought to bedпїњвАФObadiah thanked GodпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњ

вАФпїњвАФпїњNow, said Obadiah, I shall have a calf: so Obadiah went daily to visit his cow.

SheвАЩll calve on MondayпїњвАФon TuesdayпїњвАФon Wednesday at the farthestпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњ

The cow did not calveпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњnoпїњвАФsheвАЩll not calve till next weekпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњthe cow put it off terriblyпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњtill at the end of the sixth week ObadiahвАЩs suspicions (like a good manвАЩs) fell upon the Bull.

Now the parish being very large, my fatherвАЩs Bull, to speak the truth of him, was no way equal to the department; he had, however, got himself, somehow or other, thrust into employmentпїњвАФand as he went through the business with a grave face, my father had a high opinion of him.

вАФпїњвАФпїњMost of the townsmen, anвАЩ please your worship, quoth Obadiah, believe that вАЩtis all the BullвАЩs faultпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњ

вАФпїњвАФпїњBut may not a cow be barren? replied my father, turning to Doctor Slop.

It never happens: said Dr.¬†Slop, but the manвАЩs wife may have come before her time naturally enoughпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњPrithee has the child hair upon his head?пїњвАФadded Dr.¬†SlopпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњвАФ

вАФпїњвАФпїњIt is as hairy as I am; said Obadiah.пїњвАФпїњвАФпїњObadiah had not been shaved for three weeksпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњWheu -¬†- u -¬†-¬†-¬†- u -¬†-¬†-¬†-¬†-¬†-¬†-¬†- cried my father; beginning the sentence with an exclamatory whistleпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњand so, brother Toby, this poor Bull of mine, who is as good a Bull as ever pпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњssвАЩd, and might have done for Europa herself in purer timesпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњhad he but two legs less, might have been driven into Doctors Commons and lost his characterпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњwhich to a Town Bull, brother Toby, is the very same thing as his lifeпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњвАФ

LпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњd! said my mother, what is all this story about?пїњвАФпїњвАФпїњ

A cock and a bull, said YorickпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњAnd one of the best of its kind, I ever heard.
